findings.title: Bare catch silently swallows directory read errors in discoverSkills
findings.description: discoverSkills() catches all errors from readdirSync (including permission denied, broken symlinks, OS-level failures) without binding the error parameter and returns an empty array. If builtinSkillsDir or userSkillsDir is misconfigured or inaccessible, the caller (SkillRegistry.loadAll) silently gets zero skills with no indication anything went wrong. This is indistinguishable from a legitimately empty directory. Per CLAUDE.md error_handling rules, `catch (e) {}` and silent swallowing are absolutely forbidden.
findings.suggestion: Inject or accept a logger parameter. At minimum, log a warning with the directory path and error message: `logger.warn('Failed to read skills directory', { baseDir, error: (err as Error).message })` for the first catch. Ideally, distinguish 'directory does not exist' (ENOENT, acceptable) from other errors (permission denied, etc.) which should propagate.

findings.title: Bare catch silently drops invalid skills during discovery
findings.description: When loadSkillFromDir throws (invalid YAML, missing required fields, file read error), the error is silently swallowed with no logging and no error parameter binding. A skill with a subtle frontmatter typo (e.g., 'naem' instead of 'name') silently disappears from the registry. The comment 'Skip invalid skills during discovery' documents the intent but does not constitute handling. Per CLAUDE.md, `catch (e) {}` is a forbidden pattern -- a bare catch without even binding the error is strictly worse.
findings.suggestion: At minimum bind the error and log: `catch (err) { console.warn('Skipping invalid skill', { dir: entry.name, error: (err as Error).message }) }`. Better yet, collect failed skills and return them alongside successful ones (e.g., `{ skills: Skill[], errors: { dir: string, error: Error }[] }`) so the caller can surface issues to operators.

findings.title: Entire skills-engine package has no consumer in any app
findings.code_snippet:
export { buildSkillsContext, extractLayer1, formatLayer1Summary, formatLayer2Body } from './context-integration'
export { discoverSkills, loadSkillFromDir, parseSkillMd, validateMetadata } from './skill-loader'
export { SkillRegistry } from './skill-registry'
findings.description: Grep for `@proj-airi/skills-engine` across the entire monorepo returns only the package's own package.json. No app (stage-tamagotchi, stage-web, server) imports this package. No service registers it via injeca. No IPC handler, event listener, or scheduled job calls into SkillRegistry or buildSkillsContext. The entire package is dead-on-arrival orphan code. Per CLAUDE.md integration rules: 'Code written but not reachable from any entry point is dead-on-arrival. Every new module must trace to at least one live entry point.'
findings.suggestion: Wire skills-engine into at least one consumer before committing. The most natural integration point is the Anima orchestrator in `apps/stage-tamagotchi/src/main/services/anima/` which already assembles system prompts. Add `@proj-airi/skills-engine` as a dependency there, instantiate SkillRegistry during service init, and call buildSkillsContext() to inject skill context into the LLM system prompt. This makes the package reachable from the Electron main process entry point.

findings.title: JSDoc claims id 'must match directory name' but this is never enforced
findings.code_snippet:
/** Unique identifier, must match directory name */
id: string
findings.description: The JSDoc on SkillMetadata.id states the id 'must match directory name', but neither validateMetadata(), loadSkillFromDir(), nor discoverSkills() verify this constraint. A skill with id 'foo' in directory 'bar/' would be silently accepted. A future maintainer reading this comment would incorrectly believe the invariant is enforced, potentially relying on it in downstream code without adding their own validation.
findings.suggestion: Either enforce the constraint in discoverSkills() by comparing skill.metadata.id against entry.name, or soften the JSDoc to '/** Unique identifier, conventionally matches directory name */' to reflect reality.

findings.title: Skill activation state stored only in memory (Map), not persisted
findings.code_snippet: private entries = new Map<string, SkillRegistryEntry>()
findings.description: SkillRegistry stores all skill activation state (`active: boolean`) in a closure-scoped Map. If the Electron process restarts, all activation state is lost -- every skill reverts to `active: false`. This is the recurring 'in-memory state in Electron services' pattern flagged in previous reviews (task-9 mcp-hub, task-7). Per CLAUDE.md: 'Business state in memory' is a forbidden pattern; the alternative is 'Persist to DB'. Skill activation is a user preference that must survive restarts.
findings.suggestion: Add a persistence layer for activation state. Options: (1) Accept a `storage` adapter in SkillsEngineConfig that persists Map<skillId, boolean> to SQLite/better-sqlite3, consistent with the existing context-engine storage pattern. (2) At minimum, add save/load methods that serialize the active state to a JSON file. The `activate()` and `deactivate()` methods should trigger persistence. loadAll() should restore previously-active state after discovery.

findings.title: Silent catch in discoverSkills has no test for malformed SKILL.md
findings.description: The discoverSkills function silently swallows errors when a subdirectory contains a malformed SKILL.md (e.g., invalid frontmatter, missing required fields). No test exercises this code path. The bare catch block with no logging is also a code quality concern (silent error swallowing), but from a test perspective the critical issue is that this error resilience behavior is entirely untested. A regression that turns this into a rethrow would break discovery for directories containing any malformed skill file, and no test would catch that.
findings.suggestion: Add a test in skill-loader.test.ts that creates a directory with a malformed SKILL.md (e.g., missing required frontmatter fields) alongside a valid one, calls discoverSkills, and verifies: (1) the valid skill is returned, (2) the malformed one is skipped, (3) no error is thrown.

findings.title: parseSkillMd error messages omit filePath parameter
findings.code_snippet:
throw new Error('SKILL.md content is empty')
  ...
  throw new Error('SKILL.md is missing YAML frontmatter')
  ...
  throw new Error(`SKILL.md metadata is missing required fields: ${missing.join(', ')}`)
findings.description: parseSkillMd receives a filePath parameter but none of its three throw statements include it in the error message. When scanning dozens of skill directories, these errors are undebuggable without knowing which file failed. The filePath is already available as a parameter -- it just needs to be included. Per CLAUDE.md error_handling rules: 'Include context in errors: what failed, why, what input caused it'.
findings.suggestion: Include filePath in all error messages: `throw new Error(`SKILL.md content is empty: ${filePath}`)`, `throw new Error(`SKILL.md is missing YAML frontmatter: ${filePath}`)`, and `throw new Error(`SKILL.md metadata missing required fields [${missing.join(', ')}]: ${filePath}`)`.

findings.title: SkillRegistry.getById() leaks mutable internal entry
findings.description: SkillRegistry.getById() returns a direct reference to the internal Map entry. Because SkillRegistryEntry.active is mutable (no readonly modifier), any caller can bypass activate()/deactivate() by writing entry.active = true directly. Similarly, getAll() spreads the values array but each element is still the same mutable reference. This breaks the registry's encapsulation -- the activate/deactivate methods become optional suggestions rather than enforced state transitions.
findings.suggestion: Return a defensive copy: return entry ? { ...entry, skill: { ...entry.skill } } : undefined. Alternatively, mark SkillRegistryEntry fields as readonly and expose only an immutable view type (ReadonlySkillRegistryEntry) from the getter, keeping the mutable version internal to the class.

findings.title: No boundary integration test for skills-engine package
findings.description: While the package has good unit tests (skill-loader.test.ts, skill-registry.test.ts, context-integration.test.ts), there is no integration test proving the full flow: loadAll() -> activate skills -> buildSkillsContext() produces valid output that could be injected into an LLM system prompt. The unit tests test each component in isolation. Per CLAUDE.md integration rules: 'Every component that produces or consumes data across a boundary must have at least one test proving the connection works.'
findings.suggestion: Add an integration test that: (1) Points SkillRegistry at the real `skills/companion-persona` and `skills/proactive-care` directories bundled with the package. (2) Calls loadAll(). (3) Activates companion-persona. (4) Calls buildSkillsContext(registry.getAll().map(e => e.skill), registry.getActive()). (5) Asserts the output contains Layer 1 summaries for both skills and Layer 2 body only for companion-persona.

findings.title: No logging infrastructure in skills-engine package
findings.code_snippet:
import * as fs from 'node:fs'
import * as path from 'node:path'
import matter from 'gray-matter'
findings.description: The entire skills-engine package contains zero logging. No import of `@guiiai/logg`, no `useLogg` calls anywhere. Key operations that should log: skill discovery start/complete with count, individual skill load success, skill activation/deactivation, validation failures. Without logging, debugging skill loading issues in production requires adding temporary console.log statements -- which are themselves forbidden by CLAUDE.md.
findings.suggestion: Add `@guiiai/logg` as a dependency. Create a logger in each module: `const logger = useLogg('skills-engine:loader')`. Log at INFO level: skill discovered, skill loaded, skill activated/deactivated. Log at WARN level: skill validation failure (with details), directory not found. Log at DEBUG level: raw metadata parsed, file path resolved.

findings.title: loadSkillFromDir is async but uses only synchronous I/O
findings.description: The function is declared async and returns Promise<Skill>, but its body only uses fs.readFileSync (synchronous). There is no await or asynchronous operation inside the function. This creates an unnecessary promise wrapper and misleads callers into thinking the function performs async I/O. Similarly, discoverSkills (line 96) is async but only calls synchronous fs methods plus loadSkillFromDir (which itself is sync in practice).
findings.suggestion: Either commit to async I/O (fs.promises.readFile) or drop the async keyword. Mixing async function signatures with synchronous bodies adds cognitive overhead and misleads about blocking behavior.

findings.title: Synchronous fs calls inside async functions block event loop
findings.description: Both `loadSkillFromDir` (line 84: `fs.readFileSync`) and `discoverSkills` (line 99: `fs.readdirSync`, line 113: `fs.existsSync`) are declared `async` but use synchronous `fs` APIs. This is misleading -- callers expect these to be non-blocking (they return Promises), but they actually block the Node.js event loop. With many skill directories, `discoverSkills` would block the main thread for the duration of all file reads. In the Electron main process, this would freeze the UI.
findings.suggestion: Replace synchronous calls with their async equivalents: `fs.promises.readFile`, `fs.promises.readdir`, `fs.promises.access` (or `fs.promises.stat`). Since the functions are already async, the change is straightforward.

findings.title: Token budget claims (<200 / <5000 tokens) are not enforced
findings.code_snippet:
/**
 * Layer 1: Lightweight metadata summary (<200 tokens).
 * Used for initial system prompt injection to let LLM know available skills.
 */
findings.description: SkillLayer1 JSDoc claims '<200 tokens' and SkillLayer2 claims '<5000 tokens' (line 36). These budget constraints also appear in context-integration.ts line 18. However, no code enforces token counting or truncation. If a skill has a very long description or body, the budget will be exceeded silently. These comments will mislead maintainers into thinking the system handles token budgets automatically.
findings.suggestion: Either add token-counting validation (e.g., a rough word-count check in parseSkillMd or formatLayer1Summary), or reword the comments to 'Target: ~200 tokens (not enforced at runtime)' to set correct expectations.

findings.title: activate() and deactivate() are near-identical methods
findings.code_snippet:
activate(id: string): boolean { ... entry.active = true ... }
  deactivate(id: string): boolean { ... entry.active = false ... }
findings.description: The activate() and deactivate() methods are structurally identical -- the only difference is the boolean value assigned to entry.active. This is 10 lines of near-duplicate code.
findings.suggestion: Extract a private setActive(id, active) helper method. The current code is also acceptable given the mild duplication level.

findings.title: SkillMetadata.category typed as plain string, not a union
findings.code_snippet:
/** Skill category for grouping */
  category: string
findings.description: SkillMetadata.category is typed as plain string but semantically represents a finite set of skill categories. The type system cannot prevent typos or invalid categories. The same pattern applies to SkillMetadata.id (should be at minimum a non-empty string brand) and SkillMetadata.version (should be a semver-shaped type or branded type).
findings.suggestion: Define a SkillCategory literal union type (e.g., type SkillCategory = 'coding' | 'research' | 'analysis' | 'creative' | string & {}) to provide autocomplete while allowing extension.

findings.title: All interfaces in types.ts lack readonly modifiers
findings.code_snippet:
export interface SkillMetadata {
  id: string
  name: string
  category: string
  version: string
findings.description: All seven interfaces are defined with mutable fields. In a Functional Core architecture, data flowing through pure functions should be immutable. Without readonly modifiers, any function receiving a Skill object can silently mutate fields, leading to subtle bugs where shared references are unexpectedly modified.
findings.suggestion: Add readonly to all interface fields. For SkillRegistryEntry, keep the internal mutable version for the registry class and export a Readonly<SkillRegistryEntry> view type for consumers.

findings.title: Change is horizontal-only: no end-to-end path exercisable
findings.code_snippet:
export { buildSkillsContext, extractLayer1, ... } from './context-integration'
export { SkillRegistry } from './skill-registry'
findings.description: This task delivers a complete internal library (loader, registry, context builder) but no vertical slice. There is no way for a user to exercise this code: no IPC handler, no UI setting, no CLI command, no event listener calls into skills-engine. Per CLAUDE.md: 'Every task delivers a thin, working end-to-end path. Horizontal-only tasks are a forbidden task structure.'
findings.suggestion: Deliver a minimal vertical slice: (1) Register SkillRegistry in the Electron main process via injeca. (2) Add an IPC handler (e.g., `skills:getAll`, `skills:activate`) that the renderer can call. (3) Wire buildSkillsContext() into the system prompt assembly pipeline.

findings.title: SkillLayer2 type exported but never used anywhere
findings.description: The `SkillLayer2` interface is defined and exported from the package but never referenced by any code in the codebase. It duplicates the fields already present in the `Skill` interface. The `formatLayer2Body` function takes a `Skill`, not a `SkillLayer2`. This is dead code that adds confusion about the intended type hierarchy.
findings.suggestion: Either remove `SkillLayer2` if it serves no purpose, or refactor `formatLayer2Body` to accept `SkillLayer2` as its parameter type to establish a clear Layer 1 / Layer 2 type boundary.

findings.title: activate/deactivate return false for both not-found and already-in-state
findings.description: activate() and deactivate() return false when the skill ID is not found in the registry. There is no way for the caller to distinguish 'ID not found' from success. Callers cannot detect typos in skill IDs.
findings.suggestion: Consider throwing an error for the not-found case: `throw new Error(`Skill not found: ${id}`)` or return a discriminated union `{ found: boolean, activated: boolean }`.

findings.title: Optional array fields not validated for element types
findings.description: parseSkillMd validates that required fields are strings, but optional array fields (allowedTools, dependencies, tags) are only checked with Array.isArray(). The array elements could be numbers, objects, or null -- gray-matter parses YAML which can produce any type. Since these arrays are typed as string[], silently accepting non-string elements violates the type contract.
findings.suggestion: Validate array element types: if (Array.isArray(parsed.data.allowedTools) && parsed.data.allowedTools.every((t: unknown) => typeof t === 'string')). Better yet, define Valibot schemas for SkillMetadata -- Valibot is the project standard for validation.

findings.title: No test for dependencies field parsing in parseSkillMd
findings.description: The parseSkillMd function has explicit handling for the optional 'dependencies' array field (lines 59-61), but no test in skill-loader.test.ts exercises this path. The 'tags' and 'allowedTools' optional fields are tested via VALID_SKILL_MD, but 'dependencies' is never included in any test fixture.
findings.suggestion: Add a test case with a SKILL.md that includes a dependencies field in the frontmatter and assert that skill.metadata.dependencies equals the expected array.

findings.title: Error path tests use bare .toThrow() without message/type check
findings.description: Four error path tests assert only that an error is thrown (.toThrow()) without verifying the error message or type. This means any error (including unexpected ones like TypeError from null access) would satisfy the assertion.
findings.suggestion: Use .toThrow(/specific message pattern/) or .toThrow('exact message') to verify the correct error is thrown.

findings.title: Reference to 'agentskills.io standard' is unverifiable
findings.code_snippet:
/**
 * SKILL.md frontmatter metadata (YAML section).
 * Follows agentskills.io standard.
 */
findings.description: The JSDoc claims the interface 'Follows agentskills.io standard'. This external standard reference is not linked, documented, or verifiable within the codebase. If the standard does not exist, this comment would be misleading.
findings.suggestion: Either add a URL link to the standard so maintainers can verify conformance, or remove the reference if it is aspirational rather than factual.

findings.title: No test for SkillRegistry.loadAll() idempotency (re-load)
findings.description: The loadAll() method calls this.entries.clear() at the start, which is an important state transition: previously loaded skills (including any that were activated) are wiped. No test verifies that calling loadAll() a second time correctly resets state.
findings.suggestion: Add a test: load skills, activate one, call loadAll() again, verify the previously-activated skill is now inactive (active: false).

findings.title: loadAll() JSDoc duplicates class-level JSDoc verbatim
findings.code_snippet:
/**
 * Load and register all skills from built-in and user directories.
 * User skills override built-in skills with the same ID.
 */
findings.description: The loadAll() JSDoc repeats the exact same information as the class-level JSDoc (lines 5-8): 'User skills override built-in skills with the same ID.' Duplicated documentation increases maintenance cost.
findings.suggestion: Remove the override detail from the loadAll() JSDoc, keeping it only at the class level.

findings.title: Five trivial JSDoc comments restate method names on self-documenting code
findings.description: Five methods (getAll, getById, activate, deactivate, getActive) have JSDoc that simply restates the method name and signature in natural language. These comments have negative ROI: they add maintenance burden without providing information beyond what the method signature already communicates.
findings.suggestion: Remove the trivial JSDoc from these five methods, or replace them with comments that add value (e.g., document return semantics or edge cases).

findings.title: Three identical Array.isArray checks could use a loop
findings.description: Three consecutive blocks each check Array.isArray on an optional field and assign it to metadata. Per project convention of 'three similar lines > premature abstraction', the current form is acceptable. Only recommended if more optional array fields are added.
findings.suggestion: Current code is acceptable. If the pattern grows beyond 3 fields, consider looping over field names.

findings.title: Inline comment explains 'what' but not 'why' for silently skipping errors
findings.description: The comment '// Skip invalid skills during discovery' describes what the code does (skips), which is already apparent. It does not explain WHY silently skipping is the desired behavior.
findings.suggestion: Rewrite to explain intent: '// Resilience: one malformed SKILL.md should not prevent loading other skills'. Also add logging.

findings.title: discoverSkills could use Result pattern to surface partial failures
findings.code_snippet: export async function discoverSkills(baseDir: string, source: SkillSource): Promise<Skill[]> {
findings.description: discoverSkills currently returns Skill[] and silently drops any skills that fail to load. Per CLAUDE.md architecture guidance (Functional Core), a Result/Either pattern would allow the function to return both successful skills and structured error information.
findings.suggestion: Return a result type like `Promise<{ skills: Skill[], errors: Array<{ dir: string, error: Error }> }>` so callers can handle partial failures explicitly.

findings.title: Registry test recreates identical config object in every test case
findings.description: The same SkillRegistry instantiation with identical config appears 12 times across the test file.
findings.suggestion: Add a createRegistry() factory helper at the top of the describe block to reduce 3 lines to 1 per test.

findings.title: SkillLayer2 is unused -- type never constructed or referenced
findings.description: SkillLayer2 is defined and exported but never instantiated or used anywhere in the codebase. The formatLayer2Body() function works directly with Skill objects, not SkillLayer2. Currently dead code that may confuse consumers about when to use Skill vs SkillLayer2.
findings.suggestion: Either use SkillLayer2 as the return type of a toLayer2() extraction function (parallel to extractLayer1), or remove it to avoid confusion.

findings.title: buildSkillsContext not tested with all skills active
findings.description: The buildSkillsContext tests cover partial active skills, no active skills, and no skills at all. Missing: the case where all skills are active. While this is a minor gap, it would catch regressions where the concatenation of multiple Layer 2 bodies has formatting issues.
findings.suggestion: Add a test: buildSkillsContext([SKILL_A, SKILL_B], [SKILL_A, SKILL_B]) and verify both Layer 2 bodies appear in the output.

positive_observations: Clean functional core design: parseSkillMd, validateMetadata, extractLayer1, formatLayer1Summary, formatLayer2Body are all pure functions with clear input/output -- excellent for unit testing

Good test coverage for each module: skill-loader.test.ts (11 tests), skill-registry.test.ts (12 tests), context-integration.test.ts (10 tests) using real temp directories and real file I/O rather than mocks
Well-defined type hierarchy (SkillMetadata, Skill, SkillLayer1, SkillRegistryEntry) with clear separation between metadata layers
User skills properly override built-in skills with same ID via simple Map insertion order, with test coverage for this edge case
Input validation on SKILL.md frontmatter is thorough: checks for empty content, missing frontmatter, missing required fields with descriptive error messages
Build configuration (tsdown, vitest, tsconfig) follows existing monorepo patterns correctly; vitest.config.ts properly added to root workspace config
JSDoc is used consistently on all exported interfaces, types, and public functions
The Layer 1 / Layer 2 conceptual model is well-documented across types.ts and context-integration.ts -- clear two-tier system prompt injection strategy
No TODO, FIXME, HACK, PLACEHOLDER, or other forbidden comment patterns found
No mock violations detected: all tests use real filesystem operations with temp directories, direct class instantiation, and pure function calls
All source functions are short (<45 lines) with low cyclomatic complexity (max CC=5), well within project thresholds
Consistent use of early returns and guard clauses keeps nesting depth at 2 or below in all source files
SkillSource is a proper 2-value literal union type ('builtin' | 'user') rather than a plain string -- good discriminator
SkillRegistry uses private Map with encapsulated state -- good class-level encapsulation pattern consistent with existing codebase
The context-integration module is a textbook example of small pure functions composed together (extractLayer1 -> formatLayer1Summary -> buildSkillsContext)
Edge cases well covered in tests: nonexistent directories, empty directories, missing frontmatter, empty content, nonexistent skill IDs
